serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of sautéed pork with chili peppers as a side dish in a Singaporean meal is approximately 150 g, equivalent to about 3–4 tablespoons of the stir-fried mixture.

nutrient_source.title: Sautéed Pork With Chili Peppers — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Sautéed Pork With Chili Peppers (cr_id G00010075). All per-100g values are taken verbatim from the HPB record. Saturated fat data not reported by HPB; estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for cooked pork loin (NDB 10062) as a secondary reference.

gi_source.title: N/A — negligible carbohydrate content; GI not applicable
gi_source.source_match_notes: Glycemic index is not applicable for this food. Carbohydrate content is 0.0 g per 100 g as reported by HPB. Foods with negligible carbohydrates do not produce a meaningful glycemic response and are not assigned a GI value. Per the Veritas critical rule for low/zero-carbohydrate foods, glycemic_index is set to 0 (numeric sentinel for not-applicable in this context), gi_category is 'not_applicable', and glycemic_load_per_serving is 0.0 by calculation.

hypertension.key_factor: Extremely high sodium content at 1050 mg per 100 g, far exceeding the 600 mg per 100 g upper threshold, with very low potassium at 47 mg per 100 g providing no meaningful counterbalance.
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should avoid or strictly limit this dish; if consumed, use low-sodium soy sauce, reduce added salt, and keep portion size to no more than 50–75 g to limit total sodium intake to under 600 mg per serving.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Zero carbohydrates and zero sugar per 100 g mean this dish will not raise blood glucose; high protein content (19.1 g per 100 g) supports satiety and glycaemic stability.
type2_diabetes.guidance: This dish is suitable for people with Type 2 Diabetes from a glycaemic perspective; however, soy sauce marinades may contain hidden sugars so label-checking is advised, and the dish should be paired with low-GI carbohydrate sides to balance the overall meal.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Sodium at 1050 mg per 100 g significantly elevates cardiovascular risk; cholesterol at 89 mg per 100 g and estimated saturated fat contribute to atherogenic load.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: People with cardiovascular disease should limit this dish due to its very high sodium content and moderate saturated fat; if consumed, portion size should be restricted to 50–75 g and preparation should use minimal oil and low-sodium soy sauce to reduce overall cardiovascular risk.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Pork is rich in arachidonic acid (omega-6 fatty acid) which amplifies inflammatory cytokine pathways; high sodium at 1050 mg per 100 g worsens RA-related tissue swelling; chili peppers are nightshade vegetables that may trigger joint inflammation in sensitive individuals.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid this dish or substitute pork with anti-inflammatory protein sources such as salmon or tofu, and should use low-sodium soy sauce to reduce inflammation-promoting sodium load.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Sautéed Pork With Chili Peppers (辣椒炒肉, Là Jiāo Chǎo Ròu) is a popular Chinese stir-fried dish widely consumed in Singapore and across the Chinese diaspora in Southeast Asia. The dish is prepared by quickly sautéing sliced or diced pork — typically lean pork loin or belly — in a hot wok with fresh or dried chili peppers, garlic, and a soy-sauce-based seasoning. The cooking method is high-heat stir-frying, which produces a slightly charred, savoury exterior on the pork while retaining moisture inside. The flavour profile is bold, spicy, and umami-rich, with the chili peppers providing heat and the soy sauce contributing deep saltiness. The texture is tender-firm with a glossy sauce coating. Per 100 g, the dish provides 171 kcal, 19.1 g protein, 10.5 g fat, and 0 g carbohydrates, making it a high-protein, zero-carbohydrate side dish. Sodium is extremely high at 1050 mg per 100 g, which is the primary clinical concern for individuals with hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The dish is suitable for people with Type 2 Diabetes from a glycaemic standpoint due to its negligible carbohydrate content, but the high sodium and saturated fat content make it unsuitable for those managing blood pressure or heart disease without significant modification. It is commonly served as a side dish alongside steamed rice in a Singaporean Chinese meal context, typically at lunch or dinner. The dish is not halal as it contains pork.

notes: Carbohydrate_g is confirmed zero per HPB Singapore Foodo database (G00010075); this is consistent with a pork-and-chili dish prepared without starchy ingredients or thickening agents — confirmed zero. Sugar_g is confirmed zero per HPB; no added sugar in the standard preparation — confirmed zero. Dietary_fibre_g is confirmed zero per HPB; the chili pepper quantity in this dish is insufficient to contribute measurable fibre at the dish level as reported — confirmed zero. Saturated_fat_g (3.5 g) is estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy cooked pork loin (NDB 10062) as HPB does not report this entry; this is an approximation. Monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid profile, full vitamin panel, phosphorus, zinc, selenium, and moisture data for this specific HPB dish entry are not available in the HPB Foodo database or USDA FoodData Central as of 2026-04-16. Glycemic index is not applicable as this food contains negligible carbohydrates (0.0 g per 100 g); gi_category set to 'not_applicable' and glycemic_index set to 0 as a numeric sentinel value per Veritas scoring requirements for zero-carbohydrate foods. The dish is not halal as it contains pork. Chili peppers are nightshade vegetables; individuals with rheumatoid arthritis who are sensitive to nightshades should exercise caution.
